content.problemStatement:
Given an integer NNN, output the NthN^{th}Nth term in the Fibonacci Series.

The Fibonacci Series is: `0 1 1 2 3 5 8...`

Complete the function `fib()` function to solve this problem using **recursion**.


Input Format

The input contains a single integer NNN.


Output Format
Output the NthN^{th}Nth term in the Fibonacci Series.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases (1 ≤ T ≤ 10). Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 30) representing the position in the Fibonacci series whose value has to be printed.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the Nth term of the Fibonacci series on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There is one test case. N=1 corresponds to the first element of the series, which is 0.
One test case with N=2. The second element of the Fibonacci series is 1.
One test case with N=7. The series up to the 7th term is 0,1,1,2,3,5,8,13; thus the 7th term is 13.

solution.approach: The Fibonacci series is defined as F(1)=0, F(2)=1 and F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2) for n>2. A naive recursive solution repeats the same sub‑problems many times, leading to exponential time. To keep the recursive spirit while achieving linear time, we store already computed values in a memo table (array or map). The recursive function first checks the base cases, then looks up the memo table; if the value is not present it computes it recursively, stores it, and returns it. Because each index from 1 to N is computed at most once, the time per test case is O(N) and the extra space is O(N) for the memo table.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the requested position (max 30). Overall O(T * N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the memoization table (max 30 entries).
